test_text.title: Pieter's Application for a Current Account at the Bank

audioscript.text: Section 1, you will hear a man talking to the customer services manager of a bank.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will see that there is an example that has been done for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: On this occasion only, the conversation relating to this will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning, I'd like to open a bank account, please.
audioscript.speaker: PIETER

audioscript.text: Certainly.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: If you'd like to take a seat, I'll just get some details from you.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: It won't take long.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Thanks.
audioscript.speaker: PIETER

audioscript.text: Is it a current account or a deposit account you wanted?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: A current account.
audioscript.speaker: PIETER

audioscript.text: The man wishes to apply for a current account, so current has been written in the space.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now we shall begin, you should answer the questions as you listen.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully, and answer questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning, I'd like to open a bank account, please.
audioscript.speaker: PIETER

audioscript.text: Certainly.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: If you'd like to take a seat, I'll just get some details from you.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: It won't take long.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Thanks.
audioscript.speaker: PIETER

audioscript.text: Is it a current account or a deposit account you wanted?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: A current account.
audioscript.speaker: PIETER

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I've got the application form here then. We have different types.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I see you've got our leaflet there.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I've decided on the one called 'Select'.
audioscript.speaker: PIETER

audioscript.text: Right, that's fine.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: So, first of all, can I have your full name please?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, it's Pieter Henes.
audioscript.speaker: PIETER

audioscript.text: That's PIETER.
audioscript.speaker: PIETER

audioscript.text: Is it HENNES?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Uh, only one N actually, it's a less common spelling of the name.
audioscript.speaker: PIETER

audioscript.text: Oh, right.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: OK, and what's your date of birth please?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: The 27th of the 1st, 1973.
audioscript.speaker: PIETER

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And will this be a joint account?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: No, just myself.
audioscript.speaker: PIETER

audioscript.text: OK, fine.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And where are you living, Mr Henes?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: 15. Riverside.
audioscript.speaker: PIETER

audioscript.text: Is that all one word?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: PIETER

audioscript.text: Exeter?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: PIETER

audioscript.text: How long have you been at your present address?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Er, Is it more than two years?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Ah, just two weeks actually.
audioscript.speaker: PIETER

audioscript.text: I only arrived in the country a month ago.
audioscript.speaker: PIETER

audioscript.text: I'm from Holland.
audioscript.speaker: PIETER

audioscript.text: Oh, that's fine.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: But we normally ask for a previous address in that case.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Oh, yes.
audioscript.speaker: PIETER

audioscript.text: Well, it's Rielsdorf 2.
audioscript.speaker: PIETER

audioscript.text: That's RIELSDORF 2, Utrecht.
audioscript.speaker: PIETER

audioscript.text: Holland.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: OK, thank you.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Do you have a daytime telephone number?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, I think the number at my office is 6 0 6 2 9 5.
audioscript.speaker: PIETER

audioscript.text: Um, just a minute, I'd better check.
audioscript.speaker: PIETER

audioscript.text: Oh, no, sorry, 6 1 6.
audioscript.speaker: PIETER

audioscript.text: I'm not used to it yet.
audioscript.speaker: PIETER

audioscript.text: Would you like my home number too?
audioscript.speaker: PIETER

audioscript.text: Yes, please.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Are they both local numbers?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: PIETER

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Right. And your occupation?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, I'm in Britain as a project manager, but that's not my main job.
audioscript.speaker: PIETER

audioscript.text: I'm an engineer by profession.
audioscript.speaker: PIETER

audioscript.text: I see.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I think I'll put that then, it's shorter.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Now we usually ask for a piece of information which we can use to check your identity for security reasons, you know, if you phone us.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Like, erm, my wife's first name?
audioscript.speaker: PIETER

audioscript.text: Mother's might be better, it's less likely to be known.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: OK, hers is Siti.
audioscript.speaker: PIETER

audioscript.text: Siti?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, SITI.
audioscript.speaker: PIETER

audioscript.text: It's Indonesian.
audioscript.speaker: PIETER

audioscript.text: Fine.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And how much would you like to open your account with?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: We usually ask for a minimum sum of £50 that's about 75 euros.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, I'm going to transfer 2,000 euros from my Dutch account, uh, just till I get paid.
audioscript.speaker: PIETER

audioscript.text: Uh. In fact, I wanted to ask you about that.
audioscript.speaker: PIETER

audioscript.text: What's the best way to do it?
audioscript.speaker: PIETER

audioscript.text: It depends which bank you're with.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: It's the Fransen Bank in Utrecht.
audioscript.speaker: PIETER

audioscript.text: OK, fine.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I'll check that in a minute.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: If we have links with them, we can do a direct transfer, but it's not a big problem either way.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Let's see, how often would you like to receive statements?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I haven't really thought. Um, what's the usual thing?
audioscript.speaker: PIETER

audioscript.text: It's up to you.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Some people like them weekly.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Oh, no, that's too often.
audioscript.speaker: PIETER

audioscript.text: Can I have them sent once a month?
audioscript.speaker: PIETER

audioscript.text: Yes, that's fine.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Is there anything else?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I was thinking of registering for your internet service at some stage.
audioscript.speaker: PIETER

audioscript.text: Oh, yes.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Would you like me to send you information about that?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Oh, please, yes.
audioscript.speaker: PIETER

audioscript.text: And would you like to receive information about the bank's other services?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Insurance, loans, anything like that?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Hmm. I don't think so, thanks.
audioscript.speaker: PIETER

audioscript.text: That's OK, then.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And one last thing, if you agree.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: OPENING A BANK ACCOUNT

test_text.title: A Tour of Rosewood: Home of Sebastian George

audioscript.text: Section 2, you will hear a guide giving visitors to a country house information about the house and gardens.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 13.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 13.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Welcome everybody to the lovely house and gardens of Rosewood, once the home of the famous writer Sebastian George.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: He bought the house in 1902, although he had first seen it two years earlier.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: At that time, the owners let it out to a tenant, because George was too slow making up his mind to buy it.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: When it came back on the market, there was no hesitation, and he bought it immediately.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For 9,300 pounds, even though the house had no bathroom, no running water upstairs, and no electricity.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: When he came here, he'd been married for 10 years.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: During that time, he'd become one of the most famous writers in the English speaking world.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: His professional success was enormous.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But his personal life wasn't as successful.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: He was no longer on speaking terms with his brother, and had been devastated by the death at the age of 7 of his elder daughter Josephine.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Moving to Rosewood allowed the family to start a new life.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: George regarded Rosewood as a pure example of a traditional country house of this part of England, and did some of his most successful writing here.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The house and its grounds became the family haven, and their escape to privacy and quiet.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The walls and the mullioned windows were built of the local sandstone.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The tiles on the roofs and the bricks of the chimney stack were baked from local clay, and the wooden structures inside came from oak trees which grow around here.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 14 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 14 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now, please look at the map I've given you of the house and gardens.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We're here, at the information center.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Follow the path marked with the arrow, and the first area you come to is the Orchard on your left.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As you go further down the path, there's the kitchen garden on the right, and as you go round the first sharp corner, you will find to your left an area where different types of pear tree have been planted.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As well as some lovely flowers, and this is known as Pear Alley, designed by George himself.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Next to this is the greenhouse, where some exotic plants and fruits are grown.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Follow the path round the second corner, and on your right you will see the entrance to the Mulberry Garden, with its 500 year old tree.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Past the Mulberry Garden, follow the path until you reach the front of the house.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I suggest you spend a good hour wandering around this lovely building.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A guide takes visitor groups round every two hours.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If you would like to purchase any of George's books or other souvenirs, then leave the house by the side entrance, where you will find our shop, which is situated between the house and the garage, which contains the magnificent old Rolls-Royce car which used to belong to George.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I expect by this time you may also be in need of a rest and some refreshment.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Most visitors are, so why don't you visit the tea room on the far side of the garage?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If you have time, there is a lovely walk down towards the River Dudwell.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For me, this is the best part of the estate.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This isn't on the map.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But it is all clearly signposted.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You cross the field, which spreads along the banks of the river.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In spring, this area is well worth a visit.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Spend a minute or two watching the water pass by underneath, as you cross the footbridge, and then continue along the river walk through the woodland.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: On a hot summer's day, the trees along this path provide welcome shade.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Eventually, you come to the watermill, which used to provide the electricity for the house, only about four hours every evening, in George's time.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And finally, for those of you who would like to see stunning views of the surrounding countryside, and who are a little bit more energetic, when you return from the mill, take the first turning on your left, and climb up to the viewpoint.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You won't regret it.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Enjoy your visit.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Section 2, you will hear a guide giving visitors to a country house information about the house and gardens.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 13.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 13.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Welcome everybody to the lovely house and gardens of Rosewood, once the home of the famous writer Sebastian George.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: He bought the house in 1902, although he had first seen it two years earlier.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: At that time, the owners let it out to a tenant, because George was too slow making up his mind to buy it.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: When it came back on the market, there was no hesitation, and he bought it immediately.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For 9,300 pounds, even though the house had no bathroom, no running water upstairs, and no electricity.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: When he came here, he'd been married for 10 years.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: During that time, he'd become one of the most famous writers in the English speaking world.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: His professional success was enormous.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But his personal life wasn't as successful.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: He was no longer on speaking terms with his brother, and had been devastated by the death at the age of 7 of his elder daughter Josephine.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Moving to Rosewood allowed the family to start a new life.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: George regarded Rosewood as a pure example of a traditional country house of this part of England, and did some of his most successful writing here.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The house and its grounds became the family haven, and their escape to privacy and quiet.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The walls and the mullioned windows were built of the local sandstone.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The tiles on the roofs and the bricks of the chimney stack were baked from local clay, and the wooden structures inside came from oak trees which grow around here.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 14 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen and answer questions 14 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now, please look at the map I've given you of the house and gardens.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We're here, at the information center.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Follow the path marked with the arrow, and the first area you come to is the Orchard on your left.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As you go further down the path, there's the kitchen garden on the right, and as you go round the first sharp corner, you will find to your left an area where different types of pear tree have been planted.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As well as some lovely flowers, and this is known as Pear Alley, designed by George himself.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Next to this is the greenhouse, where some exotic plants and fruits are grown.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Follow the path round the second corner, and on your right you will see the entrance to the Mulberry Garden, with its 500 year old tree.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Past the Mulberry Garden, follow the path until you reach the front of the house.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: I suggest you spend a good hour wandering around this lovely building.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: A guide takes visitor groups round every two hours.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: If you would like to purchase any of George's books or other souvenirs, then leave the house by the side entrance, where you will find our shop, which is situated between the house and the garage, which contains the magnificent old Rolls-Royce car which used to belong to George.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: I expect by this time you may also be in need of a rest and some refreshment.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Most visitors are, so why don't you visit the tea room on the far side of the garage?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: If you have time, there is a lovely walk down towards the River Dudwell.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For me, this is the best part of the estate.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This isn't on the map.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But it is all clearly signposted.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: You cross the field, which spreads along the banks of the river.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In spring, this area is well worth a visit.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Spend a minute or two watching the water pass by underneath, as you cross the footbridge, and then continue along the river walk through the woodland.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: On a hot summer's day, the trees along this path provide welcome shade.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Eventually, you come to the watermill, which used to provide the electricity for the house, only about four hours every evening, in George's time.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And finally, for those of you who would like to see stunning views of the surrounding countryside, and who are a little bit more energetic, when you return from the mill, take the first turning on your left, and climb up to the viewpoint.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: You won't regret it.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Enjoy your visit.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of section 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: When the writer Sebastian George first saw Rosewood House, he
options: thought he might rent it.
felt it was too expensive for him.
was unsure whether to buy it.

items.title: Before buying the house, George had
options: experienced severe family problems.
struggled to become a successful author.
suffered a serious illness.

items.title: According to the speaker, George viewed Rosewood House as
options: a rich source of material for his books.
a way to escape from his work.
a typical building of the region.

items: You can walk through the <input> that goes along the river bank.
You can go over the <input> and then into a wooded area.
On your way back, you could also go up to the <input>.
body.title: RIVER WALK

test_text.title: Marketing Assignment Discussion: Entertainment Industry Research

audioscript.text: Section 3, you will hear two students, Jack and Lucy, discussing a marketing assignment they are working on together.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 24.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 24.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Lucy, we really need to get working on this marketing assignment.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: We've only got five weeks left to the end of term to design it, carry it out, and then write up the results.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Sure, well, let's get started right now.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY

audioscript.text: Let's go over the instructions.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY

audioscript.text: What exactly do we have to do?
audioscript.speaker: LUCY

audioscript.text: Well, it says here we have to look at one area of the entertainment industry.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: There's a list of the different types.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: What are they?
audioscript.speaker: LUCY

audioscript.text: Uh. Music, cinema, theatre, sport, and eating out.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Is that all?
audioscript.speaker: LUCY

audioscript.text: Looks like it.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: So we choose one branch of the industry, and then we look at how two different groups of people use it.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY

audioscript.text: Is that right?
audioscript.speaker: LUCY

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: And do we have to use any particular method to get our data?
audioscript.speaker: LUCY

audioscript.text: Can we mail out a questionnaire, or do face-to-face interviews, or maybe even observations?
audioscript.speaker: LUCY

audioscript.text: Well, actually, it looks like we don't have a choice.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: We have to do telephone interviews.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: OK, so at least we don't have to waste time deciding between the different methods.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY

audioscript.text: Yeah, that's right.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Oh, and the other requirement is the number of interviewees.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Not too many I hope.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY

audioscript.text: Ten? Twenty?
audioscript.speaker: LUCY

audioscript.text: Well we have to do two groups, remember, and it looks like we have to interview 15 for each group.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: That's 30 altogether, then it's going to take ages.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY

audioscript.text: Yes, but remember we're working on this together, so we'll only have to do 15 each.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: OK, so those are all the requirements?
audioscript.speaker: LUCY

audioscript.text: Yes, looks like it.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: So, first, which area are we going to choose?
audioscript.speaker: LUCY

audioscript.text: My preference would be cinema, since that's where I spend most of my money.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY

audioscript.text: Hmm. I don't think that's such a good idea.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: I don't think there are huge differences in the market there.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: I mean, you get young and old, male and female, rich and poor, all going to the same movies.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Yeah, maybe you're right.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY

audioscript.text: Let's make it music then.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: So, what two groups will we compare and contrast?
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Male and female?
audioscript.speaker: LUCY

audioscript.text: Hmm. Most of my female friends like the same music as me.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Different age groups will be much more likely to show up differences, I think.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 25 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 25 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Yeah, I suppose you're right again.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY

audioscript.text: I'll take some notes, shall I?
audioscript.speaker: LUCY

audioscript.text: So, age groups.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY

audioscript.text: Well, what do you think?
audioscript.speaker: LUCY

audioscript.text: Maybe 25 or under for one group, and 45 or over for the other group.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY

audioscript.text: That should show up differences.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: OK, next.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY

audioscript.text: How about the kind of music they like?
audioscript.speaker: LUCY

audioscript.text: Let's give them some choices, and then we can just tick boxes.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY

audioscript.text: OK. Let's have pop, jazz, folk, easy listening,.. What else?
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Well, we should include classical.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY

audioscript.text: Some people like it, you know.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY

audioscript.text: OK. And then we should have how they listen to music.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: The medium. Right.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY

audioscript.text: Let's include radio, CD, and then I guess there's TV.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY

audioscript.text: What about concerts?
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: You know, in pubs and halls.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Oh, yeah, we should include live music of course.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY

audioscript.text: OK, we're on a roll now.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Next point could be about where they actually get their music.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: You mean like do they buy it in music shops or department stores?
audioscript.speaker: LUCY

audioscript.text: Yes, or download it from the Internet.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Right, that could be for recorded music.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY

audioscript.text: Then we need another section for live music.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY

audioscript.text: Where do they go for that?
audioscript.speaker: LUCY

audioscript.text: OK, let's say disco, pub, club, concert hall...
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Or opera house!
audioscript.speaker: LUCY

audioscript.text: And I guess we should include karaoke bars.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY

audioscript.text: Not many of them in this city.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: OK, we'll leave that out then.
audioscript.speaker: LUCY

audioscript.text: So what's left to do?
audioscript.speaker: LUCY

audioscript.text: That's it.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Well, now we can make a time scale for doing it.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

desc.constraint: NO MORE THAN THREE WORDS AND/OR A NUMBER

items: For their assignment, the students must investigate one part of the <input>.
The method the students must use to collect data is <input>.
In total, the students must interview <input> people.
Jack thinks the music preferences of <input> listeners are similar.
body.title: MARKETING ASSIGNMENT

test_text.title: The Neolithic Period in Ireland: Farming and Innovations

audioscript.text: Section 4, you will hear a lecturer talking about farming in ancient Ireland.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully, and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning everyone, last week we were looking at the hunter gatherers in Ireland, across the Irish Sea from England.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Today we're going to move on to the period between 4 and 6 thousand years ago.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Known as the Neolithic period, which is when a total farming economy was introduced in Ireland.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now, there are several hypotheses about the origins of the first Neolithic settlers in Ireland.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But most of these contain problems.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For instance, there are considerable archaeological difficulties about the theory that they came from England.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The evidence doesn't really add up.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But there are even greater practical problems about the theory that they came directly from continental Europe.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For one thing, it's not clear just how sufficient numbers of men and women could have been transported to Ireland to establish a viable population.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As you know, the hunter gatherer economy which existed beforehand was based on small scattered groups.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The farming economy would almost certainly have required much larger communities to do all the work needed to plant and tend sufficient crops to sustain them through the year.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The early farmers kept various animals including cattle and sheep.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There's also evidence of pigs, but it is possible that these could have been descended from the native wild species.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now we know from modern farming that if the level of breeding stock falls below about 300 females, the future of the species locally is at risk.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So we must assume that from the beginnings of Neolithic farming, the number of breeding sheep would have considerably exceeded 300 and the national cattle herd must have been of a similar size.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The question is how these were brought to the area, and where they came from.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's usually suggested that the Neolithic settlers used skin covered boats to transport livestock, but this method would have severely restricted the range of the colonizing fleets.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The sheer volume of animal transport necessary means it's unlikely that this livestock could have been brought from anywhere further than England.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: What about crops?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Well, two main cereal crops were introduced to Ireland during this time.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Wheat and barley, both in several varieties.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The main evidence for their presence consists of impressions on pottery, where a cereal grain accidentally became embedded in the surface of a pot before it was fired.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The grain itself was destroyed by the firing, but it left an impression on the pot, which could be studied and identified by botanists.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Let's turn our attention now to the farming technology available at that time.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Before the cereal crops could be planted, it would have been necessary to clear the forest and to break the ground by plowing.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The stone blade of a plow has been discovered during excavation in County Mayo in western Ireland.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The body of the plow would have been of wood, and could have been drawn by people.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But it's also likely that cattle were used.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now, the cultivation of crops and the husbandry of livestock brought about changes in people's lifestyle, such as the type of shelters they made.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For one thing, instead of moving from place to place, they needed permanent dwellings.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The stone axes used to chop down trees to make these dwellings were far superior to any that the Stone Age hunter gatherers used.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: To make the axes sources of suitable stone had to be found and systematically exploited.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These so-called 'axe factories' were really quarries rather than factories, as the manufacture of the axes wasn't regularly performed on the quarry site.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: However, after the axes had been chipped into shape, they needed water and sand for grinding and polishing.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So a high mountainside wouldn't have been an appropriate place for this.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So this final stage of the manufacture must have been carried out close to water, and sure enough, there's ample evidence of this at coastal sites.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now it's clear that these Neolithic axes were transported all over Ireland, as well as to Scotland and the south of England.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's not really surprising that axes from axe factories in England have also been found in Ireland.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: At the very least, this indicates that there was a link between the two islands during that period.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One of the most useful innovations of the colonizers was pottery making, which was quite unknown to Irish hunter gatherers.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The pottery was probably made by shaping clay into a ball with the hand.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And then hollowing it until the walls were the right thickness.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: After firing, the outside was often polished.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This would have helped the pots to retain water as they weren't glazed.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now we know that the clay used usually came from local sources, which suggests that manufacture was on a fairly small scale, even though thousands of fragments are usually found at Neolithic sites.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In the course of time decoration began to appear.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: At first this looked like a series of stitches and was just around the tops of the pots.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This could have been an imitation of earlier vessels which were made of leather sewn onto wood.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Then eventually pots with decoration all over became much more...
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Section 4, you will hear a lecturer talking about farming in ancient Ireland.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen carefully, and answer questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Good morning everyone, last week we were looking at the hunter gatherers in Ireland, across the Irish Sea from England.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Today we're going to move on to the period between 4 and 6 thousand years ago.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Known as the Neolithic period, which is when a total farming economy was introduced in Ireland.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Now, there are several hypotheses about the origins of the first Neolithic settlers in Ireland.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But most of these contain problems.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For instance, there are considerable archaeological difficulties about the theory that they came from England.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The evidence doesn't really add up.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But there are even greater practical problems about the theory that they came directly from continental Europe.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For one thing, it's not clear just how sufficient numbers of men and women could have been transported to Ireland to establish a viable population.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As you know, the hunter gatherer economy which existed beforehand was based on small scattered groups.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The farming economy would almost certainly have required much larger communities to do all the work needed to plant and tend sufficient crops to sustain them through the year.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The early farmers kept various animals including cattle and sheep.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There's also evidence of pigs, but it is possible that these could have been descended from the native wild species.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Now we know from modern farming that if the level of breeding stock falls below about 300 females, the future of the species locally is at risk.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So we must assume that from the beginnings of Neolithic farming, the number of breeding sheep would have considerably exceeded 300 and the national cattle herd must have been of a similar size.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The question is how these were brought to the area, and where they came from.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's usually suggested that the Neolithic settlers used skin covered boats to transport livestock, but this method would have severely restricted the range of the colonizing fleets.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The sheer volume of animal transport necessary means it's unlikely that this livestock could have been brought from anywhere further than England.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: What about crops?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Well, two main cereal crops were introduced to Ireland during this time.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Wheat and barley, both in several varieties.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The main evidence for their presence consists of impressions on pottery, where a cereal grain accidentally became embedded in the surface of a pot before it was fired.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The grain itself was destroyed by the firing, but it left an impression on the pot, which could be studied and identified by botanists.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Let's turn our attention now to the farming technology available at that time.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Before the cereal crops could be planted, it would have been necessary to clear the forest and to break the ground by plowing.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The stone blade of a plow has been discovered during excavation in County Mayo in western Ireland.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The body of the plow would have been of wood, and could have been drawn by people.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But it's also likely that cattle were used.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Now, the cultivation of crops and the husbandry of livestock brought about changes in people's lifestyle, such as the type of shelters they made.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For one thing, instead of moving from place to place, they needed permanent dwellings.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The stone axes used to chop down trees to make these dwellings were far superior to any that the Stone Age hunter gatherers used.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: To make the axes sources of suitable stone had to be found and systematically exploited.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: These so-called 'axe factories' were really quarries rather than factories, as the manufacture of the axes wasn't regularly performed on the quarry site.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: However, after the axes had been chipped into shape, they needed water and sand for grinding and polishing.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So a high mountainside wouldn't have been an appropriate place for this.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So this final stage of the manufacture must have been carried out close to water, and sure enough, there's ample evidence of this at coastal sites.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Now it's clear that these Neolithic axes were transported all over Ireland, as well as to Scotland and the south of England.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's not really surprising that axes from axe factories in England have also been found in Ireland.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: At the very least, this indicates that there was a link between the two islands during that period.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: One of the most useful innovations of the colonizers was pottery making, which was quite unknown to Irish hunter gatherers.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The pottery was probably made by shaping clay into a ball with the hand.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And then hollowing it until the walls were the right thickness.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: After firing, the outside was often polished.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This would have helped the pots to retain water as they weren't glazed.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Now we know that the clay used usually came from local sources, which suggests that manufacture was on a fairly small scale, even though thousands of fragments are usually found at Neolithic sites.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In the course of time decoration began to appear.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: At first this looked like a series of stitches and was just around the tops of the pots.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This could have been an imitation of earlier vessels which were made of leather sewn onto wood.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Then eventually pots with decoration all over became much more...
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of Section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: According to the speaker, it is not clear
options: when the farming economy was introduced to Ireland.
why people began to farm in Ireland.
where the early Irish farmers came from.

items.title: What point does the speaker make about breeding animals in Neolithic Ireland?
options: Their numbers must have been above a certain level.
They were under threat from wild animals.
Some species died out during this period.

items.title: What does the speaker say about the transportation of animals?
options: Livestock would have limited the distance the farmers could sail.
Neolithic boats were too primitive to have been used.
Probably only a few breeding animals were imported.

items.title: What is the main evidence for cereal crops in Neolithic Ireland?
options: the remains of burnt grain in pots
the marks left on pots by grains
the patterns painted on the surface of pots
